title: An Adaptive Coherent Interferometric Oscillator Based on an Optoelectronic Magnonic Parametric Oscillator
authors: Shihao Zhou, Junming Wu, Jiazhen Li, Qing Gu, Wei Zhang
abstract: We study a Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI)-based optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator (OEMPO) incorporating a YIG-loaded magnonic branch and a tunable phase-shifter branch, enabling systematic investigation of adaptive interferometric oscillator dynamics under distributed phase perturbations. Through analysis of nondegenerate OEPO mode pairs and frequency-pulling behavior, the loop free spectral range (FSR) and effective delay time were quantitatively extracted. Despite the nominally frequency-pinned parametric operation, weak frequency pulling and OEPO mode softening were observed, revealing an additional adaptive interferometric degree of freedom introduced by the MZI architecture. By comparing local and global sampling configurations, we demonstrate that the YIG branch behaves predominantly as a local dispersive resonant subsystem governed by the complex magnonic susceptibility, whereas the phase-shifter branch primarily controls the global interferometric redistribution geometry. Nevertheless, coherent recombination and adaptive regeneration within the loop produce finite cross-coupling between the two branches, resulting in partially synchronized interferometric dynamics and branch-dependent adaptive redistribution. Quantitative complex-Lorentzian analysis further reveals substantial phase-to-amplitude conversion and distinct differences between the OEO and OEPO regimes: the phase-pinned OEPO favors strongly dispersive local YIG response, while the frequency-adaptive OEO exhibits more mixed absorptive--dispersive behavior due to spectral relaxation through frequency pulling. Broadly, the present platform establishes a versatile framework for exploring adaptive nonlinear interferometric physics, coherent phase redistribution, and branch-dependent synchronization phenomena in hybrid magnonic-photonic oscillator systems.
summary: This paper characterizes an advanced optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator built around a Mach-Zehnder interferometer. By analyzing the system's response to magnetic field perturbations, the authors demonstrate that the MZI enables complex, adaptive dynamics beyond simple resonance locking. This framework is valuable for exploring nonlinear interferometric physics in hybrid magnonic-photonic systems.
main_problem: The study investigates adaptive interferometric oscillator dynamics under distributed phase perturbations using a hybrid magnonic-photonic platform.
main_result: The MZI architecture introduces an additional adaptive degree of freedom, leading to partially synchronized dynamics where the YIG branch acts locally and the phase shifter controls global geometry.
method: Analysis involves measuring frequency pulling, comparing local vs. global sampling configurations, and applying complex-Lorentzian fitting techniques.
model_or_system: The system is an MZI-based optoelectronic magnonic parametric oscillator (OEMPO) combining a YIG-loaded magnonic branch and a tunable phase-shifter branch within an OEO loop structure.
key_observables: Loop Free Spectral Range (FSR), effective delay time ($	au$), cross-susceptibility coefficient ($\kappa_{YIG	o ps}$), and phase-to-amplitude conversion ratios.
important_parameters: The system exhibits distinct behavior in the phase-pinned OEPO regime versus the frequency-adaptive OEO regime.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the cross-coupling can be separated into amplitude-mediated and phase-mediated terms, with the latter dominating the coupling mechanism.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of the setup, measurements comparing FSR determination via mode pair separation (OEPO) versus frequency pulling (OEO), and phase evolution vs. magnetic field for different sampling points.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the system by first establishing the MZI structure's role in introducing adaptive freedom, then quantifying the distinct roles of the YIG branch (local dispersion) and the PS branch (global geometry), culminating in a quantitative analysis of their cross-coupling and regime differences.
why_interesting: The work provides an excellent platform for studying open quantum systems dynamics by modeling coherent phase redistribution and adaptive nonlinear interactions between two distinct physical subsystems (magnonics and photonics).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the setup. The top shows the photonic sector consisting of E-O / O-E converters and a fiber delay. The bottom is the microwave sector consisting of: (from right to left) the mixer RF port, amplifier (Amp), band-pass filter (1730-2270 MHz), splitter, MZI (1 & 2), combiner, Amp, attenuator (Atten), Amp, 20-dB coupler, spectrum analyzer (SA). Inside the MZI: branch 1, the reference branch, consists of a microwave phase shifter (PS) with tunable phase (φps) and an Atten; branch 2, the magnonic branch, consists of a YIG sphere placed atop a microwave stripline, which can induce a resonant, dispersive phase shift upon YIG resonance (φYIG). For both branches, a...

Figure 1 illustrates the experimental setup. The cen- tral build is an OEO loop that includes an electronic sector (consisting of microwave components for amplify- ing, attenuating, band-passing, and spectral detection) and a photonic sector (consisting of E-O/O-E converters and a fiber delay). For a proof-of-concept, the photonic part can be conveniently implemented by using fiber- optic patch cords and off-the-shelf RF-over-Fiber (RFoF) systems [6]. On the right end, an electrical parametric pumping sector can be incorporated into the loop via the local oscillator (LO) port of a frequency mixer – the nonlinear electrical device – consisting of a signal gen- erator supplying a pump signal,...

FIG. 2. Two independent methods to determine the cavity FSR. (a) In the OEPO case, the FSR can be directly read out from the separation between the nondegenerate mode pairs, e.g., fp1 and fp2, yielding 18.2 MHz. The degenerate situation yields a single strong peak at fp/2. The traces are obtained at Atten = 13 dB. Red peaks: φps = −25◦, Blue peak: φps = −100◦. (b) In the OEO case, the FSR is calculated by the frequency pulling, yielding a nominal value of 15.8 MHz and an effective βMZI ≈0.87. The trace is obtained at Atten = 20 dB.

FIG. 3. Spectrum of MZI-enhanced OEPO. (a) The full spectrum by scanning the φps from φps = −180◦to 180◦, under a pump fp = 4.2 GHz at 8 dBm power. The main loop is at Atten = 10.5 dB. (b,c) Zoom-in regime showing the frequency pulling near (b) 2.4 and (c) 1.8 GHz. The softening regime is identified near φps ∈(−43◦, 43◦), corresponding to an effective locking bandwidth of ∆flock = ∆lock/(2π) ≈1.89 MHz.

(a,b) Two sampling positions in which: (a) signal- sampling after the phase-changing elements (phase-changing →signal-sampling), and (b) signal-sampling before the phase- changing elements (signal-sampling →phase-changing). C: combiner, S: splitter. (c,d,e) phase evolution as a function of the phase-shifter nominal set phase, φps, for the case in (a) of (c) phase shifter branch (CH1), (d) YIG branch (CH2), and (e) the net phase. (f,g,h) phase evolution as a function of the phase-shifter nominal set phase, φps, for the case in (b) of (g) phase shifter branch (CH1), (f) YIG branch (CH2), and (h) the net phase.
